name: Global truncation error
statement_text:
Let f denote a function Rm → Rm , assumed to satisfy a
Lipschitz condition with constant L. Let y0 ∈ Rm and z0 ∈ Rm be two input
values to a step with the Runge–Kutta method (A, b , c), using stepsize h ≤ h0 ,
where h0 Lρ(|A|) < 1, and let y1 and z1 be the corresponding output values.
Then
                         y1 − z1 ≤ (1 + hL ) y0 − z0 ,
where
                               L = L|b |(I − h0 L|A|)−1 1.
statement_latex:
\begin{lemma}
Let \( f \) denote a function \( \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^m \), assumed to satisfy a Lipschitz condition with constant \( L \). Let \( y_0 \in \mathbb{R}^m \) and \( z_0 \in \mathbb{R}^m \) be two input values to a step with the Runge--Kutta method \( (A, b, c) \), using stepsize \( h \leq h_0 \), where \( h_0 L \rho(|A|) < 1 \), and let \( y_1 \) and \( z_1 \) be the corresponding output values. Then
\[
\| y_1 - z_1 \| \leq (1 + h L^\dagger) \| y_0 - z_0 \|,
\]
where
\[
L^\dagger = L |b|^\top (I - h_0 L |A|)^{-1} \mathbf{1}.
\]
\end{lemma}
proof_text:
Proof. Denote the stage values by Yi and  " Zi , i = 1, 2, . . . , s, respectively.
From the equation Yi − Zi = (y0 − z0 ) + h sj=1 aij (f (Yj ) − f (Zj )), we deduce
that
                                            s
               Yi − Zi ≤ y0 − z0 + h0 L         |aij | Yj − Zj ,
                                                  j=1

so that, substituting into
                                                  
                                                  s
                   y1 − z1 ≤ y0 − z0 + hL               |bj | Yj − Zj ,
                                                  j=1

we obtain the result.
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
Denote the stage values by \( Y_i \) and \( Z_i \), \( i = 1, 2, \dots, s \), respectively. From the equation \( Y_i - Z_i = (y_0 - z_0) + h \sum_{j=1}^s a_{ij} (f(Y_j) - f(Z_j)) \), we deduce that
\[
\| Y_i - Z_i \| \leq \| y_0 - z_0 \| + h_0 L \sum_{j=1}^s |a_{ij}| \| Y_j - Z_j \|,
\]
so that, substituting into
\[
\| y_1 - z_1 \| \leq \| y_0 - z_0 \| + h L \sum_{j=1}^s |b_j| \| Y_j - Z_j \|,
\]
we obtain the result. \hfill $\square$
\end{proof}

To see how to use this result, consider Figures~\ref{fig:319i} and~\ref{fig:319ii}. Each of these shows the development of global errors generated by local truncation errors in individual steps. In Figure~\ref{fig:319i}, the local truncation errors are referred to the computed solution. That is, in this figure, \( \delta_k \) is the difference between the exact solution defined by an initial value at the start of step \( k \) and the numerical solution computed in this step. Furthermore, \( \Delta_k \) is the contribution to the global error resulting from the error \( \delta_k \) in step \( k \). An alternative view of the growth of errors is seen from Figure~\ref{fig:319ii}, where \( \delta_k \) is now the difference between the exact solution at \( x_k \) and the computed solution found by using an input value \( y_{k-1} \) at the start of this step exactly equal to \( y(x_{k-1}) \). As in the previous figure, \( \Delta_k \) is the contribution to the global error resulting from the local error \( \delta_k \). To obtain a bound on the global truncation error we first need an estimate on \( \delta_1, \delta_2, \dots, \delta_n \) using these bounds. We then estimate by how much \( \delta_k \) can grow to \( \Delta_k \), \( k = 1, 2, \dots, n \). The global error is then bounded in norm by \( \sum_{k=1}^n \| \Delta_k \| \). We have a bound already from \eqref{eq:110c} on how much a perturbation in the exact solution can grow. If we were basing our global error bound on Figure~\ref{fig:319i} then this would be exactly what we need. However, we use Figure~\ref{fig:319ii}, and in this case we obtain the same growth factor but with \( L \) replaced by \( L^\dagger \). The advantage of using an argument based on this figure, rather than on Figure~\ref{fig:319i}, is that we can then use local truncation error defined in the standard way, by comparing the exact solution at step value \( x_n \) with the numerically computed result over a single step with initial value \( y(x_{n-1}) \).

variables.name: |A|
variables.definition: matrix A with every term replaced by its magnitude

variables.name: |b|
variables.definition: vector b with every term replaced by its magnitude

preamble: The lemma concerns a Runge-Kutta method defined by coefficients (A, b, c). The notation |A| and |b| denotes taking componentwise absolute values. The context involves analyzing error propagation in numerical ODE solutions.

dependencies.name: Lipschitz condition in its second variable
